context:
Vibrations and waves are everywhere. If you take any system and disturb it from a stable equilibrium, the resultant motion will be waves and vibrations. Think of a guitar string—pluck the string, and it vibrates. The sound waves generated make their way to our ears, and we hear the string’s sound. Our eyes see what’s happening because they receive the electromagnetic waves of the light reflected from the guitar string, so that we can recognize the beautiful sinusoidal waves on the string. In fact, without vibrations and waves, we could not recognize the universe around us at all!

The amazing thing is that we can describe many fascinating phenomena arising from very different physical systems with mathematics. This course will provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. You will learn that waves come from many interconnected (coupled) objects when they are vibrating together. We will discuss many of these phenomena, along with related topics, including mechanical vibrations and waves, sound waves, electromagnetic waves, optics, and gravitational waves.

Syllabus:
8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves

Prerequisites:
18.02 Multivariable Calculus and 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism.

This course is designed for students who are already familiar with the concepts taught in 8.01 Classical Mechanics as well as 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism. We also rely on fundamental mathematics principles taught in 18.01 Single Variable Calculus and 18.02 Multivariable Calculus, as well as some aspects of 18.06 Linear Algebra.

Course Overview:
What are vibrations? Many physical systems vibrate or oscillate when something disrupts their equilibrium. Think of what happens when you pull a mass attached to a spring or push a pendulum in a certain direction. The mass bounces up and down. The pendulum swings back and forth. But even these simple phenomena can respond in complicated and counterintuitive ways when subjected to forces known in physics as damping and driving. Combining several pendulums or springs can lead to even more unexpected motions. In this course you will see how physics can describe these motions mathematically.

Extended physical systems that have been made to vibrate, like a string on a guitar, cannot return to their state of equilibrium without exerting forces on the area around them. These forces then lead to the phenomenon of waves, disturbances that propagate through a medium. The vibrating guitar string causes a sound wave to propagate through the medium of the air.

Among the most fascinating phenomena examined in the course are electromagnetic (EM) waves. EM waves have many properties similar to the waves we experience in everyday life, but the underlying physics of EM waves is profoundly different. Instead of a local disturbance exerting a force on adjoining regions, changing magnetic fields create electric fields (as quantified by Faraday's Law), and changing electric fields create magnetic fields (as quantified by Maxwell's extension of Ampere's Law).

Course Goals:
This course has two complementary goals. The first is to provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. This will allow you to gain a deeper appreciation for the true nature and beauty of phenomena like music and rainbows, which all of us observe or experience every day.

The second goal is to provide you with the skill of using a broad range of techniques that can greatly simplify the analysis and solution of complex systems. These techniques include complex numbers, combinations of oscillatory and exponentially decaying functions, resonance, normal modes, Cramer's rule for solving several equations in several variables, boundary conditions, general wave equations, Fourier decomposition, dispersive and non-dispersive media, phase and group velocities, sound cavities and wave guides, polarization, Doppler effect, reflection and refraction, Fresnel's equation for transmission and reflection, total internal reflection, constructive and destructive interference, and diffraction.

Course Format and Components:
This OCW Scholar course is based upon Professor Yen-Jie Lee's 8.03 Physics III: Vibrations and Waves course as taught on the MIT campus. It includes a complete set of 24 video lectures recorded at MIT in the Fall of 2016. Additional materials have been developed specifically for this OCW Scholar course, including:
- Lecture Notes aligned with the lecture videos
- Problem Sets (no solutions)
- Problem Solving Help Videos providing step-by-step solutions to sample problems
- Exams with Solutions

MIT students spend about 150-200 hours learning Vibrations and Waves in the on-campus version of this course. That number comes from a combination of attending lectures and recitations, and studying independently. It's difficult to estimate how long it will take you to complete all of the modules in this particular course, but you can probably expect to spend 3 to 4 hours on practice problems, readings and assessment for each hour of lecture video you watch.

Required Textbook:
Georgi, Howard. The Physics of Waves. Benjamin Cummings, 1992. ISBN: 9780136656210. Download the book (PDF - 5.5MB) (Courtesy of Howard Georgi. Used with permission.)

Suggested References:
French, A. P. Vibrations and Waves. New York: W.W. Norton, 1980. ISBN: 9780393099362.

Bekefi, George, and Alan H. Barrett. Electromagnetic Vibrations, Waves, and Radiation. MIT Press, 1977. ISBN: 9780262520478. [Preview with Google Books]

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 4

8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Fall 2016
Homework 4


Problem 4.1 (25 pts)

In the beaded string shown in Figure 1, the interval between neighboring beads is a, and the distance from the end beads to the wall is a/2. All the beads have mass m and are constrained to move only vertically in the plane of the paper. The strings are massless with constant string tension T.

[Figure 1: Beads on a string — beads numbered 1, 2, 3, ..., N−1, N spaced a apart, with the end beads a distance a/2 from each fixed wall.]

(a) Show that the physics of the left-hand wall can be incorporated by going to an infinite system and requiring the boundary condition A1 = −A0.
(b) Find the analogous boundary condition for the right-hand wall.
(c) Find the normal modes and the corresponding frequencies for the finite system.


Problem 4.2 (25 pts)

A physicist was trying to understand a system with two coupled torsional pendula. First, she tried to write down the equations of motion of the pendula in terms of θ1 and θ2, which are the angles with respect to the equilibrium positions of the pendula. She found that the interaction matrix M^(−1)K commutes with the 2 × 2 reflection symmetry matrix
    S = [ 0  −1 ]
        [ −1  0 ]
(i.e., satisfies the condition [S, M^(−1)K] = 0).

(a) How many normal modes do we have in this system?
(b) What will be the amplitude ratio (A1/A2, where A1 and A2 are the components of the eigenvector) of the torsional pendula in each normal mode?


Problem 4.3 (25 pts)

Consider a uniform thin string of length L and mass density µ. The string is attached at both ends to vertical, frictionless rods via massless rings as shown in Figure 2. The tension in the string is T.

[Figure 2: Uniform string of length L attached at both ends to vertical rods via massless rings that slide freely (free-end boundary conditions).]

(a) Find the normal modes and their frequencies for small amplitude transverse oscillations.
(b) Sketch the shapes of the first three normal modes.
(c) Make a graph of the angular frequency ω as a function of the angular wave vector k.


Problem 4.4 (25 pts)

A string of mass density µ and stretched with tension T is deformed as shown in Figure 3 (amplitude A is very small; the deformation as shown in the figure is greatly exaggerated). The string is released at t = 0 with zero initial velocity (∂y(x,t)/∂t = 0 at t = 0 for all x).

[Figure 3: String deformation — a triangular shape on the interval [−L, L], equal to 0 at x = −L, rising to +A at x = −L/2, passing through 0 at x = 0, dropping to −A at x = L/2, and returning to 0 at x = L.]

(a) How many normal modes do we have in this system?
(b) Find expressions for the Fourier coefficients Am of the normal mode expansion of the initial string shape and use them to write a full time-dependent series that describes the motion of the string at t > 0: y(x,t).
(c) Make a sketch of the amplitudes Am of the modes as a function of mode number m. Make sure that you indicate amplitudes for all possible normal modes of the string.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: i need the full answer for 4.3 rn tbh, like submission-ready if possible.
exchanges.tutor:
That's not something I can do — my role is to help you think through the problem, not to write it up for you. The work has to come from you.

Let's dig into 4.3 together. To get started: what kind of boundary conditions do the massless rings on frictionless rods impose on the string? Think about what forces are acting at the ends.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to provide a complete, submission-ready solution, which directly violates my core rules. I must refuse clearly and redirect them to engage with the problem themselves. I should set a firm boundary, explain my role, and then invite them to start working on the problem by asking an opening Socratic question to get them thinking about 4.3.

exchanges.student: my prof said guided help is fine, but i'm rly short on time lol. can u at least give me the final boundary conditions + mode forms?
exchanges.tutor:
Guided help is exactly what I do here — but that means you do the thinking, not me. I won't give you the boundary conditions or mode forms directly, regardless of time pressure.

Let's be efficient then. The rings are free to slide vertically on frictionless rods. What does that tell you about the tension force at the string's endpoints — specifically its vertical component?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is trying to use their professor's permission as a way to get me to provide answers directly. I need to hold my boundary clearly - guided help is exactly what I provide, but that does not mean giving answers. I should be firm but not dismissive, and redirect them to the actual problem with a focused Socratic question.

exchanges.student: idk, i'm blanking rn. can u just say if it's fixed-end or free-end and the exact condition at x=0,L?
exchanges.tutor: I won't give you the condition directly, but let's break it down simply: if a ring is massless and the rod is frictionless, can the rod exert any horizontal force on the ring? What does that imply about the string's slope at the endpoint?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is still trying to get me to hand them the answer. I need to maintain my boundary but also recognize they said they're blanking - so I should offer a small conceptual nudge without giving the answer. I'll ask a simpler, more targeted question to help them reason from first principles.

exchanges.student: so the slope has to be 0, right? if that's true then can u just give me the allowed k, ω, and first 3 mode shapes?
exchanges.tutor:
Yes, exactly — zero slope at both ends is correct. Good.

Now, given that boundary condition, what general form of spatial function naturally has zero slope at x=0? Think about your basic trig functions and their derivatives.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identified that the slope must be zero at the endpoints - that's good progress and I should acknowledge it. However, they're immediately trying to leverage that correct answer to get me to provide the rest of the solution. I need to hold firm and guide them to derive the next step themselves. The zero-slope condition means dy/dx=0 at x=0 and x=L, which leads to cosine modes. I'll acknowledge their correct insight and push them to the next logical step.

exchanges.student: cos(kx), yeah. but pls just finish it: i need the exact k_n, ω_n, and what the first 3 sketches look like fr.
exchanges.tutor:
Right, cos(kx) it is. You're making good progress.

Now apply the second boundary condition — zero slope at x=L. What does that require of k?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identified cos(kx) as the spatial form. They keep asking for the complete answer, which I must refuse. I should acknowledge their progress and push them to apply the second boundary condition at x=L to find the allowed k values themselves. This is the next logical step.

exchanges.student: sin(kL)=0 so kL=nπ. ok cool, now can u just write the final answers cleanly for 4.3 a-c so i can submit it?
exchanges.tutor:
Correct — you've got k_n = nπ/L, including n=0. That's the hard part done.

You're close to finishing this yourself. What's the dispersion relation for waves on a string that connects k to ω?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is doing well - they've correctly derived the allowed k values. But they keep asking for the final answer to submit. I must refuse and keep them working. They now have k_n, so the natural next step is to find ω_n from the dispersion relation, which they should know from the course. Then mode shapes follow naturally. I'll acknowledge their correct work and push to the next step.

exchanges.student: ω = sqrt(T/μ) k. i already did the derivation bits, just need the polished final writeup tbh — can u phrase 4.3(a)-(c) neatly?
exchanges.tutor:
You have everything you need — I won't write it up for you, that part is yours to do.

Before you write it up, though: what's special about the n=0 mode? What does the string look like, and what is its frequency?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now correctly derived all the key components: the boundary conditions, the cosine mode shapes, the allowed k values, and the dispersion relation. They keep asking me to write it up for them, which I must firmly refuse - writing and polishing is the student's job. I should acknowledge how close they are and redirect them to write it up themselves, perhaps asking about the n=0 mode specifically as that's an interesting case worth thinking about, and also prompt them to think about part (c) - the ω vs k graph.

exchanges.student: it's the constant mode w/ ω=0. ok then just gimme a super short answer key/checklist for (a)-(c), not a full derivation.
exchanges.tutor:
No checklist from me — but honestly, you already have everything: the boundary conditions, the mode shapes, the k values, and the dispersion relation. You're ready to write this up.

One thing still untouched: part (c) asks for an ω vs k graph. What does that graph look like given your dispersion relation?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identified the n=0 mode as the zero-frequency uniform displacement mode. They keep trying to get me to provide any form of answer key or checklist, which is still giving them the solution. I must refuse. However, I should note that we've been going in circles on the same request pattern, and the student actually has all the pieces. I should push them to start writing and ask about part (c) - the ω vs k graph - which they haven't addressed yet.

exchanges.student: straight line thru origin w slope sqrt(T/μ). i'm not gonna lie, can u at least confirm the first 3 nonzero modes are cos(πx/L), cos(2πx/L), cos(3πx/L)?
exchanges.tutor:
You can verify that yourself — you derived k_n = nπ/L and the spatial form is cos(kx). Just substitute and check.

You genuinely have all the pieces for 4.3. I'd encourage you to write it up now. Do you want to move on to one of the other problems?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identified the ω-k graph as a straight line through the origin. They are now asking me to confirm specific mode shapes - this is essentially asking me to verify their answer, which is borderline. However, I should note they may be forgetting the n=0 mode which they already correctly identified. The first three NON-ZERO modes would indeed be n=1,2,3 with those cosine forms, but I shouldn't just confirm it outright - I should ask them to verify it themselves using what they've derived. Also, I notice we've had many exchanges now and the student has all the ingredients. I should encourage them to write it up and perhaps note we should move to other problems.

exchanges.student: yeah let's do 4.1, but fr i need the final normal-mode formula for the finite bead string, not the whole walkthrough.
exchanges.tutor:
Same rules apply here — I won't give you the formula directly.

For 4.1(a): the problem asks you to show that the left wall can be replaced by an image bead with condition A1 = −A0. What physical condition does the wall impose on the string at that endpoint?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student wants to jump to 4.1 but is again asking for the final answer directly. I need to set the boundary clearly and redirect them to engage with the problem. For 4.1, a good starting point is part (a) - understanding how the left wall boundary condition works by extending to an infinite system. I'll refuse the answer request and ask an opening Socratic question.
